name: arrow
summary: Better dates and times in Python — timezone-aware by default, human-friendly API, and natural language parsing.

story.problem: Python's datetime module is powerful but verbose and error-prone — especially with timezones, parsing, and humanizing. Getting 'now in UTC' requires three lines; parsing 'next Monday' is not built-in at all.
story.mental_model: Arrow wraps Python datetime into a single object that's always timezone-aware. You use arrow.now() instead of datetime.now(timezone.utc). Shifting time uses .shift(hours=+3). Converting to string is .format() or .humanize() ('3 hours ago').
story.when_to_use: Any application dealing with dates across timezones, relative time displays ('posted 2 days ago'), date parsing from strings, date range iteration, or when you want less boilerplate than stdlib datetime.
story.when_not_to_use: High-frequency numerical date computations (use numpy datetime64 or pandas Timestamp which are faster). Arrow adds overhead per operation.

alternatives.name: pendulum
alternatives.reason: Similar API but stricter, better DST handling

alternatives.name: dateutil
alternatives.reason: Powerful parsing and relative dates, stdlib-compatible

alternatives.name: datetime
alternatives.reason: Zero dependency, fine for simple UTC-only work

nodes.label: Arrow
nodes.summary: The main datetime object — timezone-aware always

nodes.label: arrow.get()
nodes.summary: Parse from string, timestamp, datetime, etc.

steps.label: Create and manipulate dates
steps.code:
import arrow

now = arrow.now('UTC')           # 2024-01-15T10:30:00+00:00
now_local = arrow.now('US/Eastern')  # auto timezone-aware

past = now.shift(days=-7)        # one week ago
future = now.shift(months=+2, days=+3)

print(now.format('YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm:ss ZZ'))
print(past.humanize())           # 'a week ago'
print(future.humanize())         # 'in 2 months'

steps.label: Parse from string
steps.code:
dt = arrow.get('2024-01-15 10:30', 'YYYY-MM-DD HH:mm')
utc = dt.to('UTC')
new_york = dt.to('America/New_York')
tokyo = dt.to('Asia/Tokyo')
print(new_york.format(), '→', tokyo.format())
